title: Is AI Algorithmic Trading Creating New Market Risks?
author: tauru_ai
selftext:
As AI algorithms take on a larger role in trading, there’s a growing conversation about the potential risks they introduce. A recent article from the London School of Economics delves into whether these trades might destabilize the market due to their speed and complexity.

Traditional investors are concerned that AI-driven trades could create a new kind of volatility, where the market responds to signals too quickly for human traders to react. However, others argue that these algorithms provide liquidity and efficiency, reducing bid-ask spreads.

What do you think? Could the automation and speed of AI lead to unforeseen risks, or will they continue to benefit the market?

comments.author: No-Consequence-1779
comments.body: Do you mean like how they traded before hft and algo trading … ai is not being used as you would think.

replies.author: tauru_ai
replies.body:
Not necessarily, HFT existed before the AI era.
I know exactly how it works, but I'm mainly talking about the easiest of algo trading in human language using AI.
No code required at all

replies.author: No-Consequence-1779
replies.body:
Yes. You probably see a post about ai 1-2x a week.  Pre ‘AI’ , which is just LLMs ; math is used. No one in htf is using a LLM. Not only does it add tremendous latency, it is non deterministic. 

Can an LLM work ? Probably. Is it necessary? No.  

I’ve actually done this for crypto trading via the Coinbase api. Once I disconnected the LLM calls , because I could send time series data to it or do the math locally, orders of magnitude faster, removing it had zero impact. 

This is because it’s going to come down to math to determine signals. The app will do it or the LLM will try.  

It is an interesting exercise though. It shows integrating the LLM APIs are simple and opens the door for better problem sets to solve.

comments.author: Accomplished-Fan9568
comments.body: They are for sure easier to manipulate for now
